speaker: interviewer
text: --an interview with Ruth Foster. And we're in London, England. And this is tape number 2. You were saying that your grandmothers had just-- had passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had-- no, they had died, really, quite a long time before. And I was in Berlin. And my parents lived in the Judenhaus. They were about--

speaker: interviewer
text: So once your parents were in the Judenhaus, you went back to Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Berlin. I carried on with my studies--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which were not-- which was very sad because we had-- for instance, in the Jewish hospital, we had some people of Polish origin, Polish Jews. And in '38, there was a pogrom that all Polish Jews were sent back to Poland. And so they emptied the hospital. Nearly half of the patients were Polish Jews. And they didn't ask whether they were ill or whether recovering or whether they were before or after operations. They just had to go. And in, in furniture vans and on lorries, they had just taken all these people away. And that opened our eyes. Up till then, it was thought of-- still, we knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were cushioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were cushioned. And we-- it didn't register that much until this started, until they'd taken the patients away. And the whole atmosphere had changed. And the war broke out. This-- already, the war had broken out. And we had air raids in Berlin right from the word go. Nearly every second night, we had to go into the shelters. And this carried on until one, one day in December, I got a telephone call-- not a telephone call. Couldn't-- Jews were not allowed to, to have telephones or radios anymore in Germany at that time. I got a telegram that-- from my parents-- that there would be-- this was the beginning of December, 1941-- that they would be deported on the 10th of December, 1941, to Riga, to Latvia, for war-important work. And we were allowed-- so I should come home. So I volunteered to go with my parents. We were allowed to take a hundredweight of clothes with us. And how carefully we weighed everything, that we had not an ounce more, or not a gram more than the hundred--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that each or between--

speaker: interviewee
text: Each of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, I must say again that neighbors, at night, they had jumpers knitted for us, you know, warm clothing that was all put into these suitcases. At that time, the Jews already had their own identity cards with a big J on it. And we got-- the women got the additional name of Sarah. So I was Ruth Sarah Heilbronn. My mother was Caroline Sarah Heilbronn. And the men got the extra name of Israel. My father was Wilhelm Israel Heilbronn. So wherever you gave the name, even if you didn't look Jewish-- but you had to give your correct name-- Ruth Sarah Heilbronn, Wilhelm Israel Heilbronn--

speaker: interviewer
text: They knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so the authorities knew that here we got a Jew. So we had suitcases with our names on it, with Riga on it, and the date, you know, also with Israel and Sarah. And we allowed, each of us, to take a rucksack and hand luggage. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the day of the 10th of-- on the morning of the 10th of December, '45-- is-- '41, it was a Shabbat. As I told you before, my parents were very Orthodox. We had to go to the town hall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, we were about eight of us. Only eight of us were deported. We were the first ones-- a family by the name of Grunberg and their daughter, Gerda; and an uncle and aunt of mine, Josef and Rosa Heilbronn, which had no children. We went to town hall and had to leave our luggage there, the hundredweight, which we pushed on a handcart to the town hall-- we had to leave it there-- and stood there. And it was bitter cold. And we were marched to the railway station. And one of the policemen were accompanying us. And this policeman, I must say, he cried all the way. I mean, this-- he-- his name was [GERMAN]. I mean, this is relevant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He cried all the way that he had to deliver us to the Nazis. This, this Policeman [GERMAN] had also arrested my father in November, 1938, during the Kristallnacht. And I was told after the war that he said to my father, Wilhelm, don't walk between me as-- between the two policemen.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two policemen who arrested my father, yes? They had to handcuff them. So he said, we are not handcuffing you. Walk in front of us. You are no criminal. And we walk behind you. Then he had to go to, to the-- to the prison, and then sent to Buchenwald with all the others. So this [GERMAN], he had to take us to Osnabruck, which was a province town, like the province. We lived in the province of Osnabruck, yes? Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to go there-- went by train. He went with us and had to deliver us there. It was in a big sports hall. And there were 200, 300 Jews from the whole area they assembled there, with their little bits of rucksack and hand, hand luggage, and so on. And we were there on straw in this town hall without any food or anything-- babies and old and young and cripples and, and invalids. And, and we were all there on this straw, lying there until we-- a day later, we were sent by train to Bielefeld. And our transport to Riga was called the Bielefeld Transport, which consisted of a thousand Jews of the area of North Rhine-Westphalia and Niedersachsen, Lower Saxony, where we came from. So a thousand Jews were assembled and transported. We went in proper trains, not in, in, in, in cattle trains, proper trains--

speaker: interviewer
text: With seats.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with seats but without any heating or anything, with one toilet for, for hundreds of people. We were just pushed in, however many they could get in. You have this picture there. We were already in the train coming from Osnabruck. And the ones from Bielefeld were joining the train. Our luggage, which we so carefully weighed and everything, we never saw again. We never got it. We only had a rucksack and hand luggage.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were these thousand people. The trains were sealed. Nobody could get in. Nobody could get out. The windows were sealed. You couldn't open any windows.

speaker: interviewee
text: The journey went from, from, from Bielefeld via Berlin, via Konigsberg, Memel, Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were on the train for quite a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on the train for three nights and four days, or vice versa. I don't know. In the meantime, the toilets had-- and you must remember, the further east we went, the colder it became. When we arrived in Riga, it was minus 30 degrees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the meantime, the toilets froze. Everything flowed over. People had heart attacks. People died. Children, babies died from cold. Like this, we arrived in Riga after four days. On the goods station, which was called Skirotava-- it was outside Riga-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] the doors were torn open by SS men with loud-hailers, rubber truncheons, and big, Alsatian dogs. They shouted, [GERMAN]. Everybody out! I mean, the old people-- and we were frozen. People had their limbs frozen. Later on, you know, they broke like matchsticks. They-- their fingers broke off like matchsticks, and their toes. And gangrene set in. And they died a terrible death-- and a stinking death because this gangrene, it smelled something horrendous. Anyways, this was our-- we were received like this in Riga. [GERMAN], everybody out, with truncheons and the dogs set up, up on us, and everything. And we had-- and it was cold. I mean, the cold-- I'm cold now. But, I mean, it's absolutely undescribable. And you had this cold wind coming from Siberia. The men had-- you know, we had on our lashes, and-- I mean the frost, you saw the frost on our lashes and our eyebrows and on our hair. We were dressed like, like Muselmann, you know, all wrapped up in the little bits we still had. And we had to be in rows of six. We had to assemble in rows of six and march. It was icy. The grounds were icy. And we had to march towards the ghetto. We didn't know. We had to just-- to assemble and to march. Always loud-hailers-- six rows of six, three rows of six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, they're with me, and the family Grunberg with their daughter, Gerda. The six of us, we were in one row. And the loud-hailers-- the SS were saying, the old people, and the sick people, and mothers with children, for those, we have provided [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wagons-- not wagons, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lorries?

speaker: interviewee
text: --lorries, warm lorries. And you don't have to walk into the ghetto. Who is not able to, go on these lorries. And these lorries never arrived. We found out later on these were these gas lorries, which they had in Treblinka as well. They had some in Riga. They gassed the people in these lorries and taken them already to the forests. I come to that a little later. They marched us-- I can't tell you now. I was told now, from my home town, they had been back to Riga, to the ghetto, and to Skirotava. It's six kilometers through the-- we went six kilometers to this ghetto. We came through a gate with watchtowers, electric wires, and old, shabby houses. This was a part of Riga. They called it the Moscow of [NON-ENGLISH], which was a suburb of Riga where the underworld lived, you know, the poor people, the gypsies, the, the down-and-outs. They had to vacate it and were given elegant houses and flats in Riga where they had taken the Latvian Jews out before we came. Before we arrived on the 14th and 15th-- I'm not clear about the date-- of December, they had killed 40,000 Latvian Jews which were in that ghetto before us. That they did it-- this in three days. They had to leave the town of Riga. They had to come into the ghetto and also the surrounding villages and towns of Riga. The Jews all had to go into the Riga ghetto. There were about 40,000 of them. They were taken away in three different Aktionen, as we called it. The SS and the Latvian SS would come in with rifles, shooting at them. And they had-- they're driven, literally driven like cattle, from the Riga ghetto in the Moscow of [NON-ENGLISH], yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the Bikernieki Forest to Rumbula, which was a forest outside [SOBBING] Riga. [CRYING] There, previously, the Russian prisoners of war [CRYING] had excavated or dug graves. And also, the men had to dig their own graves, if it wasn't enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these poor people-- I was told this by eyewitnesses-- these poor people-- and their picture is in Yad Vashem about as well-- had to undress, naked, had to sort out their clothes-- their underwear, their shoes, the, the top clothes-- all in different piles, had to stand on the edge of the-- of these mass graves and were mowed down by machine guns.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they fell into these holes. And they put chalk on it so that they should decompose quicker. And partisans and farmers, Riga-- Latvian farmers told later on survivors that the earth had been moving for days after because the people were not-- there were some which were not quite dead amongst the dead people, that the earth literally had moved [CRYING] for days afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came into these houses where they had driven those poor devils out. The wardrobes-- they were allowed to take their clothes from the town with them when they had to leave Riga. So you could see the panic.

speaker: interviewee
text: The wardrobes were thrown open. They-- at last minute, they had taken clothes out. Food was on the table. The spoons were in the soup or in potatoes. And they were frozen stiff, solid. And we came into these houses. But before we got into the ghetto, it was all covered with snow, white. And we saw pink or dark-red blotches, you know, big blotches of-- like puddles. We didn't take much notice of it, what that was, puddles of, of darker spots in the snow. Later on, we knew that they had killed some people who were not quick enough to go with the-- with the-- with-- to the forest, you know, with this Aktion. They had just shot them on the spot. And they were, of course, buried in the Jewish cemetery. But, but you-- we saw these--

speaker: interviewee
text: The blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: --puddles there, which was blood. All over the whole-- was thrown all over the ghetto, these.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were there. Now, we were how many? We were the third transport to arrive from Germany to Riga. The first one was from Cologne. The second one was from Kassel-- I know of that later. And there was one from Dusseldorf. And we were Bielefeld.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had already established themselves a little, the other-- the people, the Jewish-- German Jews who came before us. And we had an-- they had an Eldestenrat, you know, a-- somebody in charge. And, of course, we lived in these rooms. We were all uncles and aunts of mine and cousins. We were 11 in one little room in Riga. And it was-- I mean, I can't describe it. We had no food. The little bit what we had-- the food we found from these poor people, which they left behind. And we were just-- we were not given any rations or anything. But later on, it sort of got a little bit more normal. Every transport had his elders and also had an Arbeitseinsatz, people that were in charge of sending the Jews to work, to put them into groups and to go to work. And we had a little nursery for the children. And we had a hospital, which was, at first, a makeshift hospital, all on straw, and without medicines, without bandages, without anything. That's when I encountered these poor people who had frostbite-- third degree-- and lost their limbs and died under terrific, terrific pain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in there as a nurse. I mean, I was only a trainee nurse. I never finished. I never got my diploma as a nurse. We had excellent doctors, excellent doctors-- German Jewish doctors and Latvian Jewish doctors. I'll tell you in a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they live within this--

speaker: interviewee
text: They all--

speaker: interviewer
text: --ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: --lived within the ghetto. They all lived in-- within the ghetto. Why do I know more what happened in Riga was when these-- they killed these 40,000 Jewish-- Latvian Jews, they selected 3,000 young, strong men, Latvian Jews, and gave them a separate ghetto on-- opposite from us. A road separated us with electric wire and barbed wire, separated the German Jews from the Latvian Jews. They told us what was happened to their families, and so on. At the beginning, they were a little bit hostile towards us. They said, our families and friends had made-- have to make rooms. They had to be shot and killed to, to make room so that you could come from Germany into their place. But later on, we had the-- the same fate waited for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the ghetto, as I said, we lived 11 to one room, all the family. In February-- and they had taken young, strong men-- the-- they came with the transports-- were taken to a extermination camp, which was called Salaspils, which is outside Riga. And there's a big memorial now, as well, for those who died in Salaspils.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few strong, German Jewish men were taken there under the most horrific conditions. They were in barracks, which were not finished, and in this cold, without food. They were just left there to freeze and starve to death. Only a few of them survived. Amongst them, a cousin by marriage survived. And he lives now in Germany. And he's also been telling his horrific story to the Spielberg collection. And those-- they had taken the strong men away, you know, just to, to perish. But a few were left. I mean, you can't sort them all out. And in February-- this was February of '42. In the meantime, more transports came from Germany, from Czechoslovakia, and from Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents were still with you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were still with me. The last-- and I, as a nurse, went to Skirotava with sledges. Not myself, a few of us went to Skirotava with sledges to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I can't say greet-- to, to help to bring them into the ghetto, you know, on the sledges, to put little children on the sledges. And we told them not to go into these-- into these lorries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They should try to, to walk. So that's why we came with sledges. We had old people on the sledges, and so on. So that's why I know the last transport arrived in February of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: When people tell you after this-- and I met quite a few-- 'my parents were deported to Riga.' And I very tenderly asked, which months? They would say, March, February. And since January, February of '42, no transport arrived in the ghetto of Riga anymore. They were straightaway taken to the Hochwald to Rumbula and Bikernieki Forest to be executed. Why do we know as well? They had-- some of their clothes came back into the-- into the ghetto-- and their luggage. You know, we saw that's a transport from Vienna, Prague, and so on. Because the best clothes were sent to the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS had a big warehouse in Riga, where the clothes of the Jews were collected. And Jewish people had to work there and sort out the best thing to be sent back to Germany. [SIGH] That's why we know that so many had arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to the clothes that weren't sent back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went, some, to-- I don't know what, what happened to the clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But nothing went into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. No, they went to this sorting station, which was outside the ghetto where German Jews and Latvian Jews worked--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to sort them out and pack them to be sent back to Germany. Whether they sent them to, to bombed-out people, or whether they sent them to the Russian Front for the soldiers, that, that I don't know-- what happened to them. But no, no Jews came to the ghetto after February of '42. In February-- in the meantime, there were shootings. And if people were caught with something, or if you talked to the Jews-- to the Latvian Jews on the opposite side, they would shoot you. I mean, there were dead people every day lying there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In February of '42, there was a notice pinned up on, on the-- on the-- on the walls of the ghetto that the conditions are very bad in the ghetto. See, elderly people should volunteer to go, to be taken away from the ghetto to Dunamunde, which is the town that is the estuary of the-- of the Duna, which is the river which flows through Riga. And they should work in the fish-canning factory. There, they will have plenty to eat. They will be in warm billets. And they shall have it very good. But only the elderly should volunteer, which they did. They registered. And two days later, of course, they had to assemble-- what we called the Appellplatz, which is a big marketplace in the ghetto of Riga where the hangings and shootings and everything took place. They had to assemble there. They were registered. And lorries came-- not these gray lorries, which were in Skirotava, other lorries-- looked more like furniture vans. And they went on it. And we, from the nursing staff, you know, from the hospitals, we had to help the elderly people to get onto these lorries. And we were in the belief that they'll really go somewhere where they have it better than in the cold ghetto. They were going to this fish-canning factory where they had to eat and where they were in a warm place. But then afterwards, nearer the end of the-- this carried on the whole morning. Quite a few thousand of them went. Somebody tricked and said-- one of the Latvian Jews who was helping us and who was very familiar with the surrounding of Riga. He was a native of Riga. He said, they can't go in that short time to Dunamunde and back, because he noticed that the registration number of the-- of the vans, they were always the same ones that came back in a very short time. So they couldn't go to Dunamunde, there and back, in that very short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So later on, we heard that they had the same fate as the ones that were taken to the forest and were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, it's not--
